Web Design · Travel Blog · Team Project

Where Every
Destination is
a Memory

A travel blog website designed collaboratively as a first team project — exploring everything from wireframes to hi-fi, through to a complete multi-page design system in teal and gold.

🤝

This was my first experience designing as part of a team — learning to communicate decisions, divide responsibilities, and produce a unified output from multiple creative contributors.

Personal Team Project
2021
Home, Explore, Places, About
Wireframe → Hi-Fi
01
Project Overview

A Travel Blog
Built Together

BeyondTrip is a travel blog and destination guide website — a place for wanderers to discover new destinations, read travel stories, explore places by category, and get inspired to take the next trip. It was designed as a team project, which made it formative in a way that personal projects rarely are: for the first time, design decisions had to be communicated, negotiated, and justified to other people, not just executed in isolation.

The project spans five complete pages — Home, Explore, Places, About, and a newsletter section — and includes the full design process from initial wireframes through to polished hi-fidelity screens. Seeing the same layout evolve from a grey-box sketch to a fully coloured, photography-backed page was one of the most educational processes of the early design journey.

5+
Pages designed
2
Design phases (Lo→Hi)
38
Team members
1
Unified system

Deliverables

Lo-Fi Wireframes Hi-Fi Screens Homepage Explore Page Places Page About Page Style Guide Newsletter Section Footer System
02
The Team & Process

What Changes When
Design Is Collaborative

BeyondTrip was the first project designed not alone, but with a team of 38 like-minded individuals working toward a shared creative output. That scale introduced challenges that no personal project ever had — how do you maintain visual consistency when multiple people are making decisions simultaneously? How do you communicate a design direction clearly enough that someone else can execute it and produce something that feels like part of the same system?

Team Structure

A large collaborative team working toward a single unified website. The project required clear division of responsibilities and consistent communication to ensure that pages designed independently felt like they belonged to the same brand. This experience was the foundation of how I now approach handoffs, design systems, and documentation.

Shared — Visual design system and colour palette
Shared — Wireframe architecture and page structure
Shared — Hi-fidelity screen execution per page
Shared — Style guide and typography decisions
💡

The key lesson: A style guide is not optional on a team project. Without agreed colour values, typography rules, and spacing conventions documented up front, multiple designers working on multiple pages will produce multiple different-feeling products. BeyondTrip is where the importance of a shared design language became real, not theoretical.

03
Wireframes to Hi-Fidelity

Watching the Design
Come to Life

One of the most valuable aspects of this project was working through the full design process from scratch — starting with greyscale wireframes that defined structure and hierarchy, then applying the visual language on top. The before-and-after comparison makes explicit how much work colour, photography, and typography are actually doing on a finished screen.

Homepage — Lo-Fi → Hi-Fi

Lo-Fi Wireframe
→
Hi-Fi Screen
  • Wireframe establishes the full-bleed search bar hero and left-aligned headline layout — the structure is already right
  • Hi-fi adds a teal-overlaid travel photography background, turning a layout into an immersive destination-first experience
  • The Tolkien quote — "Not All Those Who Wander Are Lost" — which was planned as a text placeholder, becomes the emotional centrepiece of the hero in the final screen
  • Social icons in the wireframe bottom-left carry through to the hi-fi exactly as positioned — a rare case where the wireframe nailed placement first time

Places Page — Lo-Fi → Hi-Fi

Lo-Fi Wireframe
→
Hi-Fi Screen
  • The two-column destination card layout — image left, details right — is established in wireframe and preserved faithfully in the hi-fi
  • Wireframe uses grey boxes as image placeholders; hi-fi replaces them with real destination photography (Paris, Cape Town) that does immediate persuasive work
  • Travel service icons (Flight, Taxi, Food, Hotel) are sketched as grey squares in wireframe — they become identifiable dark icon buttons in hi-fi, giving the cards a booking-platform feel
  • The teal "Book Now" CTA button was defined in the wireframe as a full-width dark button — exactly the right call, validated by the hi-fi

About Page — Lo-Fi → Hi-Fi

Lo-Fi Wireframe
→
Hi-Fi Screen
  • The two-column text-left, image-right layout planned in wireframe translates cleanly into a split section with brand photography in the hi-fi
  • The wireframe's four-column footer (Logo, Quicklinks, References, Categories) becomes the brand's signature deep teal footer — a strong visual close to every page
  • Newsletter subscription section evolves from a simple email field wireframe placeholder into a teal card with privacy policy checkbox — adding trust signals not present in the lo-fi
  • The "About BeyondTrip" title with the brand name in teal was a hi-fi addition that wasn't in the wireframe — an example of how hi-fi reveals opportunities the wireframe couldn't anticipate
04
Key Design Decisions

The Choices That
Defined the Brand

The Teal + Gold Palette

The colour palette was one of the team's most important shared decisions. Deep teal (#012F2C) anchors the brand in the calmness and depth of nature — oceans, forests, the sky at dusk. It communicates a brand that is serene, trustworthy, and genuinely connected to the natural world it's writing about.

The golden yellow sun in the logo provides the warmth and energy that pure teal can't — it's the visual equivalent of sunlight breaking over a new destination. Together, teal and gold create the emotional combination of calm and excitement that a travel brand needs.

  • Teal used for all backgrounds, nav, footers, and CTA buttons — creates unmistakable brand consistency across every page
  • Gold/yellow reserved for the logo sun, hero badge, and select accents — every appearance feels like a deliberate moment
  • Light sky blue (#C8E8E6) used for section backgrounds — provides visual breathing room between the deep teal zones

The Tolkien Hero Quote

"Not All Those Who Wander Are Lost" — the J.R.R. Tolkien line used as the homepage hero headline is one of the most resonant decisions in the entire design. It does more emotional positioning work than a brand description paragraph ever could. It speaks directly to the traveller's identity — not a lost person, but an intentional wanderer. It sets the tone for the entire site before a single destination is shown.

Search Bar Above the Fold

Placing a destination search bar at the very top of the homepage — above the hero quote and imagery — immediately positions BeyondTrip as a utility-first travel platform, not just a passive blog. Users who know where they want to go can act immediately. Users who don't know yet scroll past it and enter discovery mode. The search bar serves both without interrupting either.

"Designing with others teaches you that a decision isn't final until it's communicated — and communication is half of design."

05
Colour & Style System

A Palette Shared
Across a Team

For a team project, the colour palette isn't just a visual decision — it's an alignment document. Every designer on the team needed to know exactly which hex values to use, and why, to ensure the final pages felt cohesive. BeyondTrip was where I first understood that a style guide is a communication tool, not just a reference sheet.

Deep Teal
#012F2C

Brand primary. Nav, hero overlays, CTAs, footer. The brand's signature colour — deep, calm, natural.

Mid Teal
#045854

Accent colour for headlines, active nav states, and section dividers. Lighter than primary, still distinctly on-brand.

Sun Gold
#F5C518

Logo sun, hero badge, pull quote accent. The warmth and energy of golden-hour travel photography.

Sky Blue
#C8E8E6

Section background tint. The colour of a calm sea at midday — creates breathing room between the deeper teal sections.

The style guide also defined Poppins as the project typeface — a modern geometric sans-serif that balances the professional requirements of a travel guide with the friendliness of a personal blog. Its rounded letterforms sit naturally against travel photography without competing with it.

06
Takeaways & Reflections

What Your First
Team Project Teaches You

Every designer has a first team project. BeyondTrip was mine. It was also where design stopped being a solo craft and became a collaborative discipline — and that shift changes how you think about every decision you make from that point on. When you design alone, the only standard you answer to is your own. When you design in a team, every decision needs to be legible, justifiable, and replicable by someone else.

📋

Style guides are essential, not optional

On a 38-person team, undocumented decisions become inconsistencies. This project taught me to treat the style guide as the first deliverable — not the last.

🔲

Wireframes are alignment tools

Lo-fi wireframes aren't just about layout — they're about getting the team to agree on structure before anyone spends time on visual execution. Disagreements at the wireframe stage cost hours. At hi-fi they cost days.

🗣️

Decision communication is a skill

Knowing why you made a design choice is not enough — you also have to be able to explain it clearly and convince others. This is a distinct skill from design itself, and BeyondTrip was where I started developing it.

🔄

Wireframes reveal, hi-fi refines

The lo-fi to hi-fi transition on this project showed how much the visual layer adds — but also that the best visual layers are built on sound structural decisions made in the wireframe phase.

✍️

Copy choices are design choices

The Tolkien quote on the homepage hero wasn't a copywriting decision — it was a brand positioning decision made in a design file. What the page says shapes what the design means.

🌱

First projects plant seeds

The habits, instincts, and lessons from a first team project follow you into every subsequent project. BeyondTrip is where collaboration, system thinking, and process discipline became part of how I design.

"Every great designer has a project that taught them to design for others, not just themselves. This was mine."

BeyondTrip
